descriptions: to become covered with ice
examples: Drivers faced a new danger as roads iced over when floodwater froze.
The pond was iced over for most of the winter.

descriptions: to consider someone or something to be involved or connected with a particular group or opinion
to feel that you can understand and share someone else’s feelings
to think that something is the same as something else or is closely related to it
examples: He didn’t seem to be able to identify with ordinary people and their aspirations.
Most of these writers identify mind with consciousness.
The Americans were anxious not to become identified with ‘colonial’ authorities.
highlighted_examples: He didn’t seem to be able to [identify] [with] ordinary people and their aspirations.
Most of these writers [identify] mind [with] consciousness.
The Americans were anxious not to become [identified] [with] ‘colonial’ authorities.

descriptions: to spend time relaxing and doing nothing
examples: Morse idled away most of Monday in his office.
The many unemployed people idle away the time.
highlighted_examples: Morse [idled] [away] most of Monday in his office.
The many unemployed people [idle] [away] the time.

descriptions: to try to make someone understand how important something is
examples: They impressed on us the need to keep the project completely secret.
highlighted_examples: They [impressed] [on] us the need to keep the project completely secret.

descriptions: to make something better than it was before, or to do something better than you did before
examples: We hope to improve on last year’s performance.
highlighted_examples: We hope to [improve] [on] last year’s performance.

descriptions: to secretly give the police or someone in authority information about someone
examples: The rule has always been that you don’t inform on a friend.
highlighted_examples: The rule has always been that you don’t [inform] [on] a friend.

descriptions: to ask someone about someone else, especially about how they are or what they are doing
examples: Rachel’s mother was inquiring after you.
highlighted_examples: Rachel’s mother was [inquiring] [after] you.

descriptions: to ask questions and examine the answers in order to find out the truth about something
examples: The committee inquired into complaints made by several prisoners.
highlighted_examples: The committee [inquired] [into] complaints made by several prisoners.

descriptions: to ask someone something
examples: ‘Are you sure?’ he inquired of Rose anxiously.
highlighted_examples: ‘Are you sure?’ he [inquired] [of] Rose anxiously.

descriptions: to keep saying very firmly that something is true, even when other people will not believe you
to say very firmly that something must happen or must be done
examples: He has insisted on his innocence from the beginning.
She insisted that we stay at her house instead of a hotel.
Some companies insist on staff undergoing regular medical checks.
The school insists that it is doing everything it can to cooperate.
You must see a doctor immediately – I insist.
highlighted_examples: He has [insisted] [on] his innocence from the beginning.
She [insisted] that we stay at her house instead of a hotel.
Some companies [insist] [on] staff undergoing regular medical checks.
The school [insists] that it is doing everything it can to cooperate.
You must see a doctor immediately – I [insist].

descriptions: to do something to stop something bad from happening
examples: There’s no foolproof way to insure against ill health while you are abroad.
highlighted_examples: There’s no foolproof way to [insure] [against] ill health while you are abroad.

descriptions: to attack a child by touching them sexually
to persuade a witness to give false information in a court of law
to prevent something from happening or developing in the correct way
to use, handle, or change something, although you have no right to do this, especially so that you damage or spoil it
examples: I saw him interfering with the smoke alarm.
Mum says I can get a job if it doesn’t interfere with my homework.
The defective gene interferes with normal body processes.
highlighted_examples: I saw him [interfering] [with] the smoke alarm.
Mum says I can get a job if it doesn’t [interfere] [with] my homework.
The defective gene [interferes] [with] normal body processes.

descriptions: to buy something, especially something that you need and will use a lot
to spend money on something in order to improve it or make it more successful
to use something such as time or effort for a particular purpose
examples: I think it’s time I invested in a new computer.
This government believes in investing in education.
You have to be willing to invest a lot of time in taking care of elderly parents.
highlighted_examples: I think it’s time I [invested] [in] a new computer.
This government believes in [investing] [in] education.
You have to be willing to [invest] a lot of time [in] taking care of elderly parents.

descriptions: to give a person or organization power, influence, or authority
to give something or someone a particular quality
examples: Nature has invested these animals with a capacity for not showing fear.
The Secret Service is invested with the authority to guard the president and his family.
highlighted_examples: Nature has [invested] these animals [with] a capacity for not showing fear.
The Secret Service is [invested] [with] the authority to guard the president and his family.

descriptions: to ask someone to go somewhere with you or do something with you
examples: I don’t mind you coming, but I didn’t expect you to invite along half your family.
highlighted_examples: I don’t mind you coming, but I didn’t expect you to [invite] [along] half your family.

descriptions: to ask someone to come to your house after you have been somewhere with them
to invite someone to your house after you have been to their house
examples: We had them over for dinner a couple of times, but they never invited us back.
highlighted_examples: We had them over for dinner a couple of times, but they never [invited] us [back].

descriptions: to ask someone to come into your house, for example after you have been out with them
examples: Did she invite you in for coffee?
highlighted_examples: Did she [invite] you [in] for coffee?

descriptions: to invite someone to go somewhere such as a restaurant or cinema with you, often because you want to start a romantic or sexual relationship with them
examples: I’m thinking of inviting him out to the theatre.
highlighted_examples: I’m thinking of [inviting] him [out] to the theatre.

descriptions: to invite someone to your house, for example for a meal
examples: They had invited some friends over for a game of bridge.
highlighted_examples: They had [invited] some friends [over] for a game of bridge.

descriptions: same as invite
examples: He’s invited us round for dinner.
highlighted_examples: He’s [invited] us [round] for dinner.

descriptions: to deal successfully with a disagreement or problem, especially by removing the last remaining difficulties
to improve something, to get rid of imperfections from something
examples: Dr Ottershaw exclaims as you step forward for the umpteenth time to iron out his difficulties.
Spreading the note on the desk, she used her hands to iron out the creases.
The liver takes the iron out and stores it for future use, then breaks the squarish molecule that is left.
There were too many much more pressing matters to iron out.
They started rehearsing the Christmas concert well in advance in order to iron out the kinks by December 20.
They’re meeting in Berlin to iron out the final details of the contract.
highlighted_examples: Dr Ottershaw exclaims as you step forward for the umpteenth time to [iron] [out] his difficulties.

descriptions: to come out of a place or thing
examples: Cheap electricity was soon issuing forth from nuclear reactors.
highlighted_examples: Cheap electricity was soon [issuing] [forth] from nuclear reactors.

descriptions: if something such as a sound or smell issues from a place, it comes out from there
examples: Acrid black smoke issued from the burning factory.
highlighted_examples: Acrid black smoke [issued] [from] the burning factory.
